explanation:
The sentence states that: 'In PCV mode it shall be possible to initiate with the push of a single button a lung recruitment procedure (RM).' This expresses a possibility: if the system is in PCV mode, then at some point (possibly immediately) it must be possible to initiate RM by pushing a single button, which is indicated by atomic proposition RMButton. Initiating the RM means that eventually RM will become active (RM).

LTL formalization:
- 'In PCV mode' = PCVMode
- 'possible to initiate with the push of a single button' = Eventually (F) after pushing RMButton, RM is activated.
- So, globally: Whenever in PCVMode, it should always be the case that if RMButton is pressed, then eventually RM becomes true:  G(PCVMode -> (RMButton -> F RM))

Thus, globally, while in PCVMode, if the recruitment maneuver button is pressed, then eventually the recruitment maneuver will be active.

If only existence of such a possibility is required (rather than required for every time RMButton is pressed), the weaker version is: G(PCVMode) -> F(RMButton & F RM). But the stronger version (with the implication per occurrence) is more faithful for an implementation requirement.

Globally, in PCV mode, if RMButton is pressed then eventually RM will be active

explanation: The sentence specifies that in PCV mode (Pressure Controlled Ventilation Mode), the initiation of a lung recruitment procedure (Recruitment Maneuver, RM) must be possible via a single button. To express this as an LTL formula, map 'PCV mode' to the atomic proposition PCVMode, and 'the push of a single button initiates RM' to the atomic proposition RMButton (button is pushed/set) and RM (RM procedure active). The requirement is about possibility: when in PCVMode, it shall be possible (not a guarantee every time, but that the system can do it) to trigger RM via one button. In LTL, possibility is often expressed as: globally in PCVMode, if the Recruitment Maneuver button (RMButton) is activated, eventually RM will hold. This is: G(PCVMode -> (RMButton -> F RM)). This means: 'whenever the system is in PCVMode, if the RMButton is pressed, then eventually RM becomes active.'

explanation: The requirement says that in PCV mode, it must be possible to initiate (start) a Recruitment Maneuver (RM) by pressing a single button. The atomic proposition PCVMode means the system is in PCV mode, and RMButton means the lung recruitment procedure can be initiated by a button press. We interpret this as: globally, whenever the system is in PCV mode, it is always possible (i.e., eventually upon request) for the user to start a Recruitment Maneuver using the RM button, which is RMButton. That is, whenever PCVMode holds, it should always be possible to perform RMButton in the future. This is expressed as G(PCVMode -> F RMButton).

explanation: The sentence specifies that when in 'PCV mode' (represented by atomic proposition PCVMode), it must be possible to initiate a lung recruitment procedure (Recruitment Maneuver - RM) with the push of a single button (represented by RMButton). 'Shall be possible to initiate with the push of a single button' means that whenever PCVMode is true, eventually the RMButton can be pressed, which will cause an RM activity. This translates to: Whenever PCVMode holds, eventually pressing the button (RMButton) leads to RM being activated (represented by F(RMButton -> RM)). Wrapping with the global 'always' operator, we get G(PCVMode -> F(RMButton & RM)), meaning: globally, whenever PCVMode is active, it is always possible to push RMButton and RM becomes active.

explanation: The sentence describes a requirement for PCV mode: it must be possible, that is, *it shall be possible to initiate ... an RM* by pushing a single button. In the atomic propositions, 'PCVMode' denotes 'Pressure Controlled Ventilation Mode (PCV)' and 'RMButton' denotes the push that initiates 'Recruitment Maneuver (RM) is set by the GUI'. 'RM' denotes when the Recruitment Maneuver (lung recruitment) procedure is active. Translating 'in PCV mode, it shall be possible to initiate with push of single button a lung recruitment procedure' into LTL: If the mode is PCV, then pressing the RMButton eventually initiates the RM. That means: Globally, whenever we are in PCVMode and the RMButton is pressed, eventually RM becomes true. Thus: G(PCVMode & RMButton -> F RM)

Globally

explanation:
The requirement states that in PCV (Pressure Controlled Ventilation) mode, it shall be possible to initiate a Recruitment Maneuver (RM) with the push of a single button. Abstracting to LTL, we look for a formula that says: Whenever the system is in PCVMode, then it is possible (i.e., there exists a run in the future) for the RMButton to be pressed, and as a result, the RM starts (i.e., Recruitment Maneuver is active).

Translating 'shall be possible to initiate' as 'from any state where PCVMode holds, it is always possible that RMButton can be pressed and then RM becomes active'. The standard way to express 'possible' in LTL is via the 'F' (eventually) operator, but since the requirement ties RM initiation to a button press, a suitable formula is: 'Globally, if we are in PCVMode, then it is always possible to (in the future) activate RM via pressing RMButton'.

So, the formula is: 
G(PCVMode -> F (RMButton & F RM))
Here:
- G: globally (always)
- (PCVMode -> ...): whenever we are in PCV mode
- F(RMButton & F RM): it is possible that, at some time, the user presses the RMButton, and, at some later time (possibly the same step), RM becomes active.
If we want to say these can occur at the same time, you could use F(RMButton & RM); if potentially RM can become active after the button is pressed (i.e., in a future state), then F(RMButton & F RM) is more general.
But since the phrase is about possibility and initiation with a single button, the minimal guarantee is that whenever RMButton is pressed in PCVMode, then eventually RM becomes active. So, including both constraints:
G(PCVMode -> F(RMButton & F RM))

explanation: The phrase 'In PCV mode' is captured by the atomic proposition PCVMode. 'It shall be possible to initiate with the push of a single button a lung recruitment procedure, termed Recruitment Maneuver (RM)' means that whenever the system is in PCV mode and the RM button is pressed (RMButton), it is possible to activate/bring about a Recruitment Maneuver (RM). In LTL, 'possible to initiate' under deterministic behavior means 'if PCVMode holds and RMButton is pressed, then eventually RM is true' (i.e., the maneuver starts). This must be true at all times, so the global (G) operator is used. Thus the formula is G((PCVMode & RMButton) -> F RM).

explanation: The phrase 'In PCV mode' constrains the property to only when PCVMode is true. 'It shall be possible to initiate with the push of a single button' means there is a button (RMButton) that can trigger the Recruitment Maneuver (RM). The core requirement is that whenever the mode is PCVMode, it must eventually be possible (i.e., possible at some point) that pressing a single button (RMButton) leads to the RM being started (RM true). We formalize this as: whenever PCVMode holds, then it must eventually be possible to press RMButton leading to RM being triggered. That is: Globally, if PCVMode holds, then eventually (F) pressing RMButton will (in the next step or at the same time) set RM active. In most LTL, 'possible to initiate with the push of a single button' is captured as (eventually, RMButton occurs and then RM becomes true). Assuming that pressing the RMButton triggers RM in the next time step, the formula can be written as: G(PCVMode -> F(RMButton & X RM)).

explanation:
The requirement says: 'In PCV mode it shall be possible to initiate with the push of a single button a lung recruitment procedure, termed Recruitment Maneuver (RM).' 

First, 'PCV mode' maps to the atomic proposition PCVMode, meaning the system is in Pressure Controlled Ventilation mode. 

'Recruitment Maneuver (RM)' is represented by RM, indicating that the RM procedure is active. 

'Initiate with the push of a single button' refers to an action available through a button press. From the atomic propositions, RMButton indicates that the Recruitment Maneuver (RM) is set via the GUI (single button press).

The phrase 'shall be possible' expresses that whenever we are in PCVMode, it is eventually possible to trigger the RM by single button push. This can be formalized as: Globally, whenever PCVMode holds, it is always possible to eventually start an RM by RMButton, and after pushing RMButton, the RM procedure is (eventually) active.

Formally:
G(PCVMode -> F(RMButton & F RM))

This means: In every state, if we are in PCV mode, then at some future point it is possible to push the RMButton, and then eventually the RM procedure will become active.

Alternatively, you could require that any time the RMButton is pressed in PCVMode, then eventually RM becomes active. Let's stick to the more permissive general statement, which follows the spirit of the requirement.

explanation: The sentence 'In PCV mode it shall be possible to initiate with the push of a single button a lung recruitment procedure, termed Recruitment Maneuver (RM)' means that whenever the system is in PCV mode, it is possible that (i.e., there exists a future point when) the user can start RM with the push of a button (represented by 'RMButton'). In LTL, 'it shall be possible' typically means possibility – i.e., that somewhere in the future (at least once) after being in PCV mode, it is possible for the button to be pressed to initiate RM. We interpret this as: whenever the system is in PCVMode, it is possible (eventually) to press RMButton, which initiates RM. We use 'G(PCVMode -> F RMButton)' to capture: always, if PCVMode is active, then at some future point, RMButton will be pressed (initiation is possible by a button).
